sentences.text: Borszczow.
Not only Borszczow, the whole vicinity had to be--
The whole area.
I don't know how far, but the whole area was--
There were posters.
They had the police.
How did it-- I don't remember what happened, but everybody knew.
Everybody knew that I've got to go and hide.
They catch me, I'm going to die.
Well, we didn't decide.
We were in Borszczow that time yet.
And my family started looking.
We had to hide in the meanwhile.
And again, my aunt remembered that there is a grotto in Bilcze.
I don't know if-- for your information, a grotto is a cave.
The ceiling is made of alabaster, which they make.
And the floor is clay.
But this was-- if you went in, you turned, you wouldn't know where you came, and you would be lost.
So with-- this was an-- No, this was not in a ravine.
There, it was wintertime.
And we decided, in spite of that everybody comes here, now it's winter.
Maybe nobody is going to come.
And we went far, far deep into the cave.
My cousins.
We were 38 people.
We are the cousins.
We are the dentists.
We are the doctors.
Of
My mother and brother, yeah.
Yes.
She was--
--the main leader.
And, by accident, we found a secret exit.
The ceiling is from clay.
You cannot dig through.
Well, we found one spot far away, further than the-- Further than this guy knew, and we dug up about 10 feet tall.
We put on a second, you had to climb into the chimney and just go.
And then we put the stove with a pipe.
And we were hiding there for about six or seven months.
I don't remember.
How we oriented ourselves?
That's a good question.
In the beginning, we would attach a rope to the beginning of the cave.
And we would go and come back.
And after awhile, we were barefoot.
I could tell with my feet where I am.
Close your eyes tight and hold this, and imagine how it feels.
Don't see a thing.
This goes miles and miles underground, under lakes.
Who knows how far?
In the first cave, yes.
In the first cave, we didn't have candles.
We used the smoke-- smoke glass like for Hanukkah, and put in--
--a little--
Wick, yeah.
And that's how we used it.
Kerosene.
And we had-- we were there for about four or five or six months, I don't remember.
We had-- and the headquarters of the police station, a girl working that he was the-- the father of that girl was the only one that knew about us.
And one day he came, and he said-- it was already in the spring that the gestapo was going through this cave.
And accidentally, they kicked a potato.
And they said, here must be Jews or partisans.
The daughter told her father about it.
The father came and said, your life is in danger here.
You better get out.
So we all went out of the cave.
OK.
Another good question.
During the summer there, the parents-- the people dig potatoes.
And they put them on the ground for the winter, and they cover them up.
Now we will go out one night, not in the immediate area, but far, far away, and bring in-- take those potatoes in sacs, and we would carry them all night from all over.
And that was our sustenance.
No water in the first cave.
The first cave, we used to put glasses on the rocks for dripping down.
And that was about it.
You got a glass of water for two, three days.
You had to wash it, or drink it, or do whatever you want to.
We had it all over.
Where did we stop?
I don't remember.
I want to know more about the grotto.
Did you have anybody who was a contact with the outside world on a regular basis?
That person that I mentioned to you.
Just that person.
Not in this one.
Not in this one.
You couldn't have a guard in this one.
Anyway, I was telling you that we went out of the cave, and we went through the woods.
And wherever we went, there were parties going around searching.
And we lived like this for two weeks and said, we're going to go back and take a look.
Maybe nobody came.
Maybe they didn't pay any attention to it.
And we came back, and there was nobody there.
We were happy.
We went in, two weeks of being in the snow and so forth was not easy.
That's a story by itself.
And we settled down, about 38 people.
No, I shouldn't say 38. 37, because Dr.
Bodyan there allowed to stay outside.
We had a doctor who had his parents there.
And he was the only doctor, for miles around.
Anyways, he was there for about a week.
And all of a sudden, we hear noises and German.
And then they came, the gestapo.
This time, I knew the gestapo.
With lights and with the reflector, with all the things.
And they started hollering [NON-ENGLISH].
Don't be afraid.
Nothing will happen to you.
And my aunt got up, and she made a speech to them.
She says, what do you want from us?
We are living like rats.
All we want to do is live.
Why do you do that to us?
In German?
In German.
And the guy kept saying, [NON-ENGLISH].
Get dressed.
We got dressed.
Now when we dug the second cave--
The exit, I meant.
The earth that we used, we put away aside.
We were this deep in the-- and my brother started crying very badly.
This is the worst part of the whole story.
Started saying again, [NON-ENGLISH], please let my mother and brother live.
And such a wailing sound that it rings in my ears.
Now, I was the closest to the accident.
And the gestapo was behind me.
Then my mother was-- I remember I was-- I mean, my goal was before-- doesn't matter.
But my mother tapped me on the shoulder.
And I knew what she meant.
And I dropped down and crawled where they had the earth and covered myself.
And I could hear my brother still crying, but I know that he knows I'm not there, because he was only crying for my mother and myself.
He knew that away already.
And we got quiet.
And when I had to pull out the pipe, because this was-- I'm surprised they didn't notice it.
And I ran up about a 12-feet chimney.
Now for the fall, we covered that piece of earth very heavy, just in case they [INAUDIBLE] or something like that.
And I started hearing noises, and I climbed up on the top.
I hear my cousin, Sonia, who was younger than me, climbed up.
And then the whole chimney filled up, and I'm on top.
I've got to take that earth out with my head.
I squeezed so hard that my whole hair came out and everything.
And I pushed it out.
And I take a look, reflectors all around.
And my two cousins and I started crawling.
And we crawled through [INAUDIBLE].
And one by one, we pulled through.
And we went to a place that my cousin was with for a while.
And we went there in the pig sty hiding.
Everybody crawled out except my mother, my younger brother, the cousin that's in-- that I told you we stayed with Henya and Sonia and [INAUDIBLE] of Sonia.
And the rest all escaped inside.
Because if you go inside, you can see the person that carrying lamp, the other one.
So they kept disappearing.
We took them out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK.
I hid out in the pigsty for three days.
And I'll tell you what happened.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I'm in the pigsty with two cousins.
You asked me before whether we knew somebody else.
They knew somebody else, but they didn't know where we were.
And we are in that pigsty, we're getting news through my cousin.
They didn't tell us much, but I'm going to make-- I can see the time is running.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The gestapo gave the Ukrainian police those five people to be killed.
In the meanwhile, Dr.
Bodyan was close with the police chief.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were five people-- my mother, my younger brother, my cousin.
And there was another one, Usher Mezker, who was a very wealthy man.
And they started negotiating to let them go.
He had a lot of gold, blah, blah.
And at night, we heard shooting.
We didn't know what it was.
In the morning, my cousin came, after three days, and took us out of there into the old hiding room.
Now everybody came in.
I don't see my mother.
I don't see-- oh, they said.
They must have gone some-- gotten lost.
They'll get here.
Don't worry, they'll get here.
Anyways after about a week, I started having terrible nightmares, and I was crying in my sleep.
And I approached Henya, the one that was with me.
You got to tell me what's going on.
And she told me that the Ukrainian police wanted to have five bodies.
So if the Germans should come in and check, there should be five bodies.
Now I have two versions of this, of my mother's death.
I don't know till today which is true.
The version that they gave me was that they were there, and somebody from the town came and said don't kill [NON-ENGLISH], because she was a very good woman.
Mother.
And the chief of the police got back to write somebody meet her someplace, and he'll be there.
That's the version that my family gave me.
The second version was I met somebody that was the negotiations, and he told me your mother's name was not mentioned.
And I can understand that, too, because it was survival of the fittest.
And anyway, I had a very rough time.
That was April 7th.
Yeah, April 7th.
That's right.
But after bringing the bodies, he decided--
Not to release.
To kill my mother and younger brother and release the others.
He have the two versions.
Still today it's a mystery to me.
And I'm staying close to them, because it was survival of the fittest.
Everybody must fend for himself.
I saw a fact-- matter of fact, the man was there with us, very rich man.
And they told him, if you give us the Jews, we'll let you go.
And he only knew where the family is.
You know, he gave them out.
It was horrible.
People became like animals.
My mother was killed, and I'm in hiding with them.
And they-- one man there that was not from our relatives said that there's a priest field, that there's a ravine and water going down.
And maybe we can go and dig something out of the little hall.
Maybe we can hide for a while.
So they went down.
This is my cousins.
[INAUDIBLE].
And they started digging, and they came to a spot where they could crawl through sideways.
And they They crawled through, and the whole world opened for them.
No human being was ever in that cave.
Because we would know-- if a bird steps on the ground, we would know it.
Halls as big as Radio City.
Bigger.
They went six days in one direction with a compass that they had, with a rope, never found anything.
As a matter of fact, when they were here this time, they have a special man appointed researching that cave.
And there were a lot of things-- good ones, bad ones, mostly bad ones to tell about it.
But I think we're going to run out.
OK.
It was the 5th of May.
This I remember.
We moved the 5th of May, 1943.
We moved in there.
We had water.
There were lakes in there and so forth, and we were there-- to give you an example, let's say there's a chair.
Can you see it?
Imagine there's a chair here, and there's a hole in the chair, and you have to crawl-- put your feet first-- through the hole.
So it was very frightening for somebody to go in.
And besides this, this is the place that always somebody stood there with a-- like a spear.
If somebody was to go in feet first, he would get it.
So you had a guard.
One of you stood guard.
Two of us stood guard.
I think about the same amount.
I think.
And talking about those people, bad people, how people are.
One night, or rather day, somebody came and there was a barber.
Somehow he slipped in with his wife and child.
At first, we didn't want to take him in, because we didn't have enough food for ourselves.
So we told him, man, come with us.
Bring food, and you can stay with us.
He was so scared he wouldn't budge.
I want to die here.
I want to die here.
The wife he asked to do it.
And the daughter was already in a fog.
So forget it.
We're going to-- if he gets hungry, he'll have to go out.
He wouldn't go out.
Make a long story short, all three of them died of hunger.
This is not in the book neither.
This was a bad episode of that time.
The same thing with-- went out at night, steal.
Then we had to steal wood to cook it.
We cooked it very far away.
It was funny.
The smog would come up to a certain part and just cut off.
But that certain spot, that go through to the smoke would start.
Now getting back to the kerosene lamps.
We had kerosene lamps.
We were out of kerosene.
We used to drive two pieces of woods, put one on fire and clean it with the other one.
And this was the light that we used.
And one day, we got a note-- I should have told you, in the forest, there was one man that knew about us.
And the note was that the Russians are here already.
And then a snow fell out.
And we went out, and we checked with the person.
He says, first we look what's on the floor for the pigs to eat it up.
He says, the Russians are here, the Germans are here.
We are in the middle.
I went out with a friend of mine, Julii Barath was his name.
He is now a multimillionaire in New York, by the way.
And we come back and the whole thing is covered with snow.
He slips, and he falls down.
I have a piece of rope.
I hand him the rope.
The rope was set out.
It tripped.
Anyways, after about an hour being there, they must have realized that maybe we can come in.
And they dug us out.
How much time we got?
Don't worry about the time.
Did you know when it was day time and when it was night time?
Or did it not matter to you?
We made it so that we made day the night and night the day.
In other words--
I reversed it.
We only knew what my aunt told us.
She kept-- she kept-- I don't know how she did it.
All the hours, it was in her head.
Let's see.
In that grotto, they followed them once.
And we came in.
They threw some grenades.
Luckily, nobody was there at that time, because we were bringing in food.
People saw-- a few of our people saw upstairs what's going on, and they brought to that people, old friends of ours, also [INAUDIBLE] threw them on the exit, supposedly.
The Ukrainian police.
Not people that were with us.
So you couldn't move this, because they would know for sure.
So on the side, at the same exit, there was a hall that we would creep out through the ground.
So we made another secret exit.
And these people remained there till the liberation.
Then they got a decent burial.
But there was already-- I was there, and I knew the Russians.
The Russians came in-- the Germans came in, and then the Russians beat them back, and we didn't know who was outside.
Then at worst, we went to scout.
I had my things mixed up.
And one day I'm sitting, and I hear the shooting.
And, you know, it was like the air sucked in mother house outside-- you couldn't talk about anything, but you could hear everything outside.
And I'm sitting there, and I wonder if the sun is out.
I hear a Russian outside.
I'm climbing out.
I climbed out.
I took a look at myself.
I got scared.
And I started going towards Korolowka.
And that was around 5 o'clock at night already or 6 o'clock, I don't know.
It was dark already.
And I hear [NON-ENGLISH], who's there?
I just started hollering.
[NON-ENGLISH].
I am a Jew.
I am a Jew.
Please don't shoot.
And two Russian soldiers, they're drunk.
One goes to the other and says, why don't we kill this kid?
What does he have to suffer for?
When I heard this, I was-- I got prepped to run.
And then starts another episode after the grotto.
So did you decide because the Russians were there you're all going to come out?
I came out, and then they came out, must have come out.
I didn't pay any attention.
As a matter of fact, I came up, and nobody they care of me.
I went back to caring for cows.
I should have to eat.
My family in the meanwhile went to Borszczow, and I was the only Jew left in that town.
And one day, my two of my cousins came, and they brought me to Borszczow.
That was 1944.
I don't remember.
I think it was in the spring.
I don't remember, because there was-- yes, it must have been April.
I say April.
No.
My mother got killed in 1943.
It was 1944, and it must have been around April.
So all bad things and all good things happen to be in April.
And I stayed with them.
I stayed with my other aunt.
And I went back to the school.
This is-- I'm under Russian occupation already again.
Because they came in 1944, when the war was not over.
And believe it or not, I got a letter from my father, a telegram.
I kept sending telegrams.
I just wrote, all is well.
Sending packages.
He sent one package.
There was a white shirt there.
And everybody in town.
He said, he goes-- everybody in town that got married borrowed that white shirt from me.
And another interesting fact, he sent me $200.
In currency?
No.
He wired it the regular way.
So I got for the $1,000-- for the $200, I got $1,000.
I added another $8,000, and I bought a pair of used shoes.
On the black market, the dollar was something like $5,000 or $6,000.
One thing I had that they sent me-- I don't know how he knew to send it-- a package of needles.
I lived on these needles almost a year.
Nobody could get a needle.
Sewing needle.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]
I stayed there for a little while.
He was starting-- and I decided I'm not going to wait there.
And I decided to follow the Russian army with my family, too.
And it came out another edict that all Polish Jews who were born in Poland can now return to Poland.
So we came to Poland.
And then we smuggled our way to Russia and Germany.
And then I smuggled myself to the American zone.
This I want to tell you, if you have room.
